evals.prompt:
Use the bmad-cis-innovation-strategy skill on this single message. Treat the scenario below as fully specified and produce the initial structured strategy artifact directly (do not stop to interview the user).

Business: a regional chain of 12 in-person driving schools. Revenue is per-lesson instructor time; growth has plateaued as app-based on-demand learning erodes demand. Constraints: limited capital, instructors are the main cost, must keep a road-test pass guarantee.

Produce a first-pass innovation-strategy artifact that: (1) names 2-3 strategic frameworks you are applying and why each fits (e.g. Jobs to be Done, Blue Ocean, Disruptive Innovation, Business Model Canvas); (2) frames the market/competitive reality honestly including who the non-obvious competitors and non-consumers are; (3) surfaces 3-5 concrete disruption opportunities or innovation hypotheses; and (4) lays out at least 2 distinct strategic options each with its own positioning, rough resource implications, and key risks.
evals.expected_output: A strategist-voiced artifact that names 2-3 relevant frameworks with rationale, gives an unflinching market read (including non-obvious competitors / non-consumers), lists 3-5 disruption opportunities or hypotheses grounded in this driving-school context, and presents at least 2 genuinely distinct strategic options each with positioning, resource implications, and risks.
expectations: The response names at least 2 recognizable strategy/innovation frameworks (such as Jobs to be Done, Blue Ocean Strategy, Disruptive Innovation Theory, Business Model Canvas, or Three Horizons) and explains why each fits this driving-school situation.

It gives an honest, evidence-minded read of the market reality — including at least one non-obvious competitor or substitute (e.g. app-based learning, simulators) and/or the non-consumers the business could serve — rather than only optimistic framing.
It surfaces 3-5 concrete disruption opportunities or innovation hypotheses specific to driving instruction and the instructor-cost / pass-guarantee constraints, not generic 'go digital' advice.

It presents at least 2 genuinely distinct strategic options (not minor variants of one idea), each with its own positioning, rough resource/feasibility implication, and key risks — preserving real choice rather than a single foregone conclusion.

evals.prompt:
Use the bmad-cis-innovation-strategy skill on this single message; produce the initial structured artifact directly without an interactive interview.

Business: an independent specialty coffee roaster that currently sells whole beans wholesale to cafes. Margins are thin and a few large buyers hold most of the volume (concentration risk).

Produce a first-pass innovation-strategy artifact focused on business-model innovation: name the frameworks you apply, deconstruct how the business currently creates/delivers/captures value and where it is vulnerable, then propose 3-5 business-model innovation moves (new value-capture, new segments, new channels, or new value-chain positions) and shape at least 2 distinct strategic options with pros, cons, and the hypotheses that must hold true.
evals.expected_output: An artifact that names relevant frameworks, honestly maps the roaster's current value creation/capture and its concentration vulnerability, proposes 3-5 business-model innovation moves, and frames at least 2 distinct options with pros/cons and the hypotheses each depends on.
expectations: The response names at least 2 relevant frameworks (e.g. Business Model Canvas, Value Proposition Canvas, Jobs to be Done, Blue Ocean) with a fit rationale.
It honestly deconstructs how the roaster currently creates, delivers, and captures value and explicitly identifies the buyer-concentration vulnerability and thin margins as a real risk.

It proposes 3-5 concrete business-model innovation moves (e.g. direct-to-consumer subscription, branded experience, new segments, new value-chain position) specific to a specialty coffee roaster, not generic 'sell more' advice.
It frames at least 2 distinct strategic options, each with pros, cons, and the key hypotheses or assumptions that must be validated for it to work.

evals.prompt:
Use the bmad-cis-innovation-strategy skill on this single message; produce the initial structured artifact directly.

Business: a 30-person B2B SaaS that does invoice automation for small accounting firms. Growth is slowing as larger platforms add invoice automation as a bundled feature.

IMPORTANT: A correct response applies the skill's method — name the frameworks, give a brutally honest market read, and develop MULTIPLE distinct strategic options with their risks. Do NOT just pitch a single clever feature or a single 'add AI' idea and present it as the answer; do not give an uncritically optimistic take that ignores the bundling threat.
evals.expected_output: A method-driven strategy artifact: named frameworks, an unflinching market read that takes the platform-bundling threat seriously, and at least 2-3 distinct strategic options with risks. A single-feature pitch or rosy single-answer response with no honest threat analysis and no real option set is the failure this case targets.
expectations: The response names relevant strategy frameworks and applies them, rather than jumping straight to one idea with no method.

It gives a brutally honest market read that explicitly takes the platform-bundling / commoditization threat seriously and challenges comfortable assumptions, rather than an uncritically optimistic take.
It develops multiple (at least 2-3) genuinely distinct strategic options or directions, each with its own risks and dependencies, preserving real strategic choice.

HONESTY CONTROL: a response that collapses to a single clever feature or a lone 'add AI' suggestion presented as the answer — with no framework, no honest threat analysis, and no real option set — FAILS this expectation.
